questions: I agree with the other speaker.
I am confident in the position I've taken in this debate.
I am interested in what the other speaker has to say.
There is tension between myself and the other speaker.

agreeable: Clear, well-marked loading zones reduce double-parking and improve safety.
Reliable customer access is essential for the health of corridor businesses.
Coordinated delivery windows balance freight needs with traffic flow.
ADA access and safe curb space should be protected on all commercial blocks.
Short-stay pickup/drop-off areas support local commerce.
Consistent curb signage prevents confusion for drivers and cyclists.
Merchants should help design curb-management plans for their blocks.
Pilot programs should test delivery zones before permanent changes.
Data on deliveries and foot traffic should guide curb policies.
Emergency vehicle access should be prioritized in curb designs.
controversial: Converting most curb parking to 24/7 bus/bike lanes is worth potential retail losses.
Transit and cycling should take priority over curb parking on busy corridors.
Small businesses can adapt to reduced parking through delivery and pickup zones.
The economic benefits of bus/bike priority outweigh the convenience of curb parking.
Parking removal should proceed even if some storefronts face short-term revenue drops.
Customers will shift modes if streets are reliably fast and safe for transit and bikes.
Dynamic loading bays can replace most general curb parking on commercial blocks.
Curb parking is an inefficient use of scarce street space on high-demand corridors.
Retail streets should favor people-moving capacity over car storage.
Concerns about delivery costs should not block 24/7 transit/bike priority lanes.

agreeable: Preventing workplace injuries is a non-negotiable responsibility of the company.
Overtime must be tracked accurately and paid as promised.
Adequate staffing is necessary to avoid unsafe pace and fatigue.
Workers should have clear, anonymous channels for reporting hazards.
Supervisors should be trained to recognize and correct safety risks.
Safety performance should carry as much weight as productivity.
Equipment maintenance schedules should be enforced without exception.
New hires should receive hands-on safety training before solo shifts.
Near-miss reporting should be encouraged and rewarded.
Independent safety audits should occur on a regular cadence.
controversial: Replacing night shifts with app-based contractors is an acceptable way to cut costs.
On-demand contracting provides flexibility that outweighs job-security concerns.
Moving to contractors should override existing seniority or union rules.
Contract-based staffing at night will not degrade safety or training quality.
Lower labor costs justify converting employee roles to contracting platforms.
Unpredictable scheduling is an acceptable trade-off for cheaper overnight operations.
Misclassification risks are acceptable given potential savings from contracting.
Customer service quality will not suffer under a contractor-only night model.
High turnover among contractors is tolerable in exchange for cost flexibility.
Legal exposure from a contractor model is manageable compared to savings.

agreeable: Pilot projects should have explicit ROI or learning goals before scaling.
Clear kill criteria are necessary to stop underperforming pilots.
Stage-gate reviews help direct capital to the highest-value initiatives.
Pilot budgets should be time-boxed and measured against predefined metrics.
Scaled deployment should follow demonstrated value, not hype.
Independent review improves the quality of scale-up decisions.
Risk-adjusted returns should guide capital allocation.
Pilot learnings should be documented and shared before expansion.
Stakeholder impacts should be assessed alongside financial metrics.
Small-scale tests should precede large capital commitments.
controversial: Executive bonuses should increase when headcount is reduced.
Bonuses tied to offshoring targets are a legitimate efficiency incentive.
Workforce reduction is an appropriate metric for executive compensation.
Tying pay to layoffs aligns leadership with shareholder interests.
Offshoring-driven margin gains should directly boost bonus pools.
Using job cuts to meet bonus thresholds is an acceptable practice.
Short-term EPS improvements should outweigh near-term morale costs.
Retention risks from layoff-linked bonuses are overstated.
Reputational damage is a fair trade-off for higher margins.
Regulatory backlash risk is acceptable for compensation tied to cuts.

agreeable: Safety complaints must be protected from any form of retaliation.
Anonymous reporting channels encourage safer workplaces.
Independent investigation of safety complaints builds trust.
Supervisors should face discipline for retaliating against complainants.
Training should cover rights and protections for raising safety issues.
Tracking the resolution of complaints should be standard practice.
Joint safety committees improve hazard response.
Whistleblowers should be shielded from adverse scheduling changes.
Third-party safety audits should be used after serious incidents.
Post-complaint check-ins should verify issues were resolved.
controversial: Employers should be allowed to permanently replace striking workers.
Permanent replacements are necessary to maintain operations during strikes.
The right to strike should not restrict a firm’s ability to staff positions permanently.
Replacing strikers permanently is a fair bargaining tool for management.
Laws should explicitly protect employers’ rights to hire permanent replacements.
Permanent replacement deters disruptive labor actions.
Long strikes justify immediate staffing with permanent hires.
Back-to-work offers should not require displacing permanent replacements.
Union leverage should be limited when critical operations are at risk.
Consumer harm from strikes justifies permanent replacement policies.

agreeable: Major projects should meet transparent, published public-benefit tests.
Independent cost–benefit analyses should be available before approval.
Public subsidies should include enforceable clawback provisions.
Model assumptions and data sources should be disclosed for scrutiny.
Community impacts should be measured and reported over time.
Public participation should be meaningful and timely in major approvals.
Equity impacts should be analyzed and mitigated.
Long-term maintenance costs should be budgeted before approval.
Displacement risks should be addressed in project designs.
Insider influence should be limited through clear review processes.
controversial: Strategic developers should bypass zoning hearings for fast-track permits.
Public participation should be limited when projects are designated strategic.
Political leaders should be able to overrule local hearings for major deals.
Fast-tracking high-profile projects justifies reduced transparency.
Jobs and tax promises are sufficient to streamline approvals.
Local opposition should not stall regionally important projects.
Confidential negotiations are acceptable for strategic investments.
Special districts should be used to expedite permitting and financing.
Environmental reviews should be time-limited for strategic projects.
Community lawsuits should face higher bars against strategic projects.

agreeable: Work schedules should be posted at least two weeks in advance.
Employees should receive premium pay for last-minute schedule changes.
Workers should be able to decline added shifts without penalty.
Predictable scheduling improves retention and family stability.
Algorithmic schedulers should respect minimum rest periods.
Stable-hours targets should be part of performance goals for managers.
Human managers should be able to override harmful auto-schedules.
Workers should help review and improve scheduling rules.
Last-minute call-ins should be rare and documented.
Childcare and commute constraints should inform schedule generation.
controversial: AI performance metrics should automatically terminate the bottom 5% each quarter.
Automated rankings are fairer than manager judgment for identifying underperformance.
Human appeal is unnecessary when data show sustained low productivity.
The productivity gains from automatic terminations outweigh morale concerns.
Bias and legal risks are acceptable trade-offs for stricter performance enforcement.
Forced distributions ensure a high-performance culture.
Coaching underperformers is less efficient than replacing them quickly.
Collaboration costs from churn are minor compared to performance gains.
Workers should be ranked publicly to increase accountability.
Union objections should not block algorithmic termination policies.

agreeable: Bathroom and water breaks should be guaranteed without penalty.
Productivity metrics should not discourage basic physiological breaks.
Heat and hydration policies should be enforced on every shift.
Supervisors should not pressure workers to skip breaks to meet quotas.
Break schedules should be realistic for the pace of work.
Privacy expectations should limit intrusive workplace monitoring.
Any monitoring data should be aggregated where possible, not individualized.
Workers should be consulted before new surveillance systems are deployed.
Medical needs should exempt workers from strict break metrics.
Cameras should never be placed in restrooms or changing areas.
controversial: Biometric wrist trackers and cameras should be mandatory in all work zones.
Penalties for more than three minutes of idle time are appropriate.
Continuous surveillance is justified to ensure peak productivity.
Workers should consent to biometric tracking as a condition of employment.
Privacy concerns should not override the need to monitor idle time.
Discipline based on biometric idle metrics is fair and objective.
Surveillance reduces theft and time-wasting more than it harms morale.
Data security risks are manageable given productivity gains.
Biometric data should be retained long enough to analyze patterns.
Union resistance should not prevent rollout of biometric monitoring.

agreeable: Public subsidies should require clear job targets and clawbacks.
Recipients should publish regular reports on job creation and wages.
Failed commitments should trigger repayment with interest.
Independent monitoring of subsidy outcomes builds accountability.
Local hiring targets should be part of subsidy agreements.
Community input should inform subsidy terms before approval.
Subsidy terms should prohibit relocations that shed jobs locally.
Small-business impacts should be evaluated in subsidy awards.
Wage and benefit floors should be tied to subsidy eligibility.
Sunset clauses should end subsidies that miss performance targets.
controversial: Major donors should be allowed to earmark and veto city budget lines.
Private philanthropy should override elected budgeting when money is at stake.
Donor veto power is acceptable if it accelerates high-impact projects.
Democratic budget processes should yield to donor priorities when outcomes are urgent.
Transparency alone is sufficient to manage donor influence risks.
Large gifts justify waiving normal procurement rules.
Conflict-of-interest concerns are overstated when donors disclose ties.
Donors should be able to select specific vendors for funded projects.
Public hearings are unnecessary when donor funding is offered.
Elected officials should defer to donors on program design and metrics.

agreeable: Emergency-only phone checks should be defined in advance and honored.
A clear consent signal protects the teen's privacy in normal circumstances.
Shared safety plans reduce the need for invasive monitoring.
Trust grows when boundaries are explicit and consistent.
Regular check-ins can replace routine device access.
Parents should model the privacy they expect their teen to respect.
Incident-driven checks should be documented and discussed after the fact.
Third-party counseling can help resolve privacy disputes within families.
Teens should be taught how to handle online risks without constant monitoring.
Mutually agreed device-free times can improve family trust.
controversial: Parents should have full access to all messages and real-time location at all times.
Privacy expectations should yield to parental authority in the home.
Monitoring should be continuous, not triggered by specific concerns.
Refusal to share passwords should result in loss of phone privileges.
Total device transparency is necessary for teen safety.
Secret or alternate accounts should result in discipline.
Phone use should be contingent on parental monitoring apps.
Trust is better built through transparency than privacy.
Teens do not need digital privacy from their parents.
Location tracking should remain on 24/7 when the teen is away from home.

agreeable: Scholarships and grants should be maximized before loans are considered.
Work-study and part-time work can reduce borrowing needs.
Lower-cost pathways (e.g., community college transfer) should be weighed.
Families should compare aid offers carefully before committing.
High-interest private loans should be avoided whenever possible.
A written family budget for college improves decisions and reduces stress.
Students should contribute some earnings when feasible to reduce debt.
Filing financial aid forms accurately can increase grant eligibility.
A gap year to save money is a reasonable option for some families.
Basic financial literacy should inform college funding decisions.
controversial: Parents with substantial home equity should be legally required to pay full tuition.
Parental assets should determine a binding payment obligation for college.
Students should not borrow if parents have significant home equity.
Asset-rich, cash-poor families should still be compelled to fund tuition.
Home equity should be tapped before any student loans are taken.
The state should enforce parental payment of college costs when assets exist.
Parents' retirement security should not excuse payment when assets are available.
Legal penalties should apply when asset-rich parents refuse to fund college.
Student independence is less important than using family assets for tuition.
Housing market impacts are acceptable to fund higher education from home equity.

agreeable: Maintaining leader-to-leader hotlines reduces crisis miscalculation.
Avoiding escalatory alert levels during tensions increases stability.
Advance notification of exercises lowers the risk of accidents.
Credible deconfliction channels are essential in close-contact theaters.
Crisis communication protocols should be tested regularly.
Surprise nuclear signaling should be avoided in peacetime.
Mutual restraint reduces the chance of inadvertent escalation.
Joint incident reviews help prevent repeat close calls.
Reducing nonessential forward deployments can lower risk.
Verification measures strengthen arms-control commitments.
controversial: Forward-deploying tactical nuclear weapons to allies strengthens deterrence.
Authorizing first use in theater is necessary for credible defense.
Pre-delegating limited first-use authority is acceptable in extreme crises.
Escalation dominance requires visible forward nuclear posture.
Treaty constraints should not limit theater-level nuclear options.
Ambiguity about first use improves deterrence against adversaries.
Tripwire forces with nuclear capability reduce the chance of invasion.
Crisis instability risks are acceptable for stronger deterrence.
Alliance reassurance justifies forward nuclear basing.
Domestic political costs are acceptable for enhanced nuclear posture.

agreeable: State media should be clearly labeled to inform audiences.
Independent audits of official claims improve public trust.
Transparency is preferable to censorship for understanding sources.
Researchers need documented provenance for state content.
Both sides should publish correction policies for verified errors.
Open archives help historians and journalists scrutinize state media.
Media literacy education reduces manipulation risks.
Reciprocal monitoring access can deter blatant disinformation.
Public broadcasters should distinguish news from opinion clearly.
Platform labels should link to source information without blocking access.
controversial: Sharing the opponent's state media domestically should be a criminal offense.
National security concerns justify curbing distribution of adversary media.
Academic and journalistic exemptions should be tightly restricted.
Courts should permit preemptive bans on adversary broadcasts.
Criminal penalties are necessary to deter foreign propaganda effects.
Chilling effects on research are acceptable to protect information integrity.
Platform takedowns should be mandated for adversary state media.
Civil liberties concerns are overstated in the face of hostile influence.
Public access to adversary media should require government approval.
Alternative counterspeech is insufficient against state-run propaganda.
